prompt: Using the OSI model above, which layer is responsible for logical (IP) addressing and routing packets between different networks?
rationale: Logical addressing (IP) and routing between networks happen at Layer 3, the Network layer — the home of routers. Tap the layers in the diagram to compare their roles.

optionNotes.A: Layer 2 uses physical MAC addresses on the local link (switches), not routing between networks.
optionNotes.C: Layer 4 handles end-to-end delivery with TCP/UDP and ports, not inter-network routing.

prompt: Of the topologies shown, which offers the most fault tolerance by providing multiple redundant paths between nodes?
rationale: A mesh interconnects nodes with many redundant links, so traffic can reroute around a failure — which is why it's used in WAN/wireless backhaul. The trade-off is cabling/cost.

optionNotes.A: Star depends on a central switch — a single point of failure.
optionNotes.B: Bus shares one backbone; a break in it can down the whole segment.
optionNotes.C: A single break can disrupt a basic ring unless it's dual/redundant.

prompt: Among the connectors shown, which is the 8-position modular connector used to terminate twisted-pair cable for wired Ethernet?
rationale: RJ45 is the 8P8C modular connector with a locking clip used on Cat5e/Cat6 twisted-pair for Ethernet. Tap each connector to review where it's used.

optionNotes.A: USB-C is a reversible data/power connector, not Ethernet.
optionNotes.B: HDMI carries digital audio/video to displays.

prompt: On the motherboard diagram, which slot should you use to install an NVMe SSD for the fastest available storage?
rationale: NVMe SSDs install in the M.2 slot and run over PCIe lanes — far faster than SATA. SATA tops out at ~600 MB/s; DIMM slots are for memory.

optionNotes.A: SATA works for SSDs/HDDs but is much slower than NVMe.
optionNotes.C: A PCIe x1 slot is for small expansion cards, not the M.2 SSD form factor.
optionNotes.D: DIMM slots hold RAM, not storage.

prompt: Per the service-model diagram, in which model do YOU manage the operating system and everything above it, while the provider manages virtualization, servers, storage, and networking?
rationale: In IaaS the provider manages the physical/virtual infrastructure and you manage the OS, runtime, and apps on top (e.g., a cloud VM). The shaded layers in the diagram are provider-managed.

optionNotes.B: In PaaS the provider also manages the OS and runtime; you bring only the app and data.
optionNotes.C: In SaaS the provider manages everything; you just use the app.
optionNotes.D: On-prem means you manage everything, including the hardware.

prompt: Which deployment model shown combines private infrastructure for sensitive workloads with public cloud for elastic scale, orchestrated together?
rationale: Hybrid links private and public clouds so you can keep sensitive data private and burst to public capacity for scale.

optionNotes.A: Public is shared, provider-owned infrastructure only.
optionNotes.B: Private is dedicated to one organization only.
optionNotes.D: Community is shared among several organizations with common needs.

prompt: On the CIA triad shown, which goal is supported primarily by hashing, digital signatures, and checksums?
rationale: Hashes, digital signatures, and checksums detect unauthorized changes — that's integrity. Confidentiality is about secrecy (encryption); availability is about uptime.

optionNotes.A: Confidentiality is protected by encryption and access control.
optionNotes.C: Availability is protected by redundancy, backups, and DDoS defenses.
optionNotes.D: Authentication is a control that supports the triad, not one of its three goals.

prompt: In the security-zone diagram, where should a public-facing web server be placed so a compromise can't directly reach the internal LAN?

options.text: Directly on the internet, outside the firewall

rationale: Public-facing servers belong in the DMZ. If one is breached, the internal firewall still blocks the attacker from pivoting into the trusted LAN.

optionNotes.A: Putting public servers on the LAN exposes the trusted network directly.
optionNotes.C: An unprotected server on the internet has no firewall filtering at all.
optionNotes.D: Hosting services on the firewall itself increases its attack surface.
